title: Effect of Grapes on Skeletal Muscle. To investigate the effect of grapes on skeletal muscles, a mouse model was used to examine gene expression in males and females fed either a grape-enriched or a bland diet over 2.5 years. The results show sex-specific changes in gene expression within muscle tissue, with females exhibiting more pronounced alterations that trend toward a male-like phenotype. Up-regulation of genes such as Ahsg, Alb, Apoa1, and Arg1, together with down-regulation of Camp, Lcn2, and Irf4, suggested improved muscle function, a finding further supported by pathway analysis. A total of 25 differentially expressed genes associated with muscle health were identified, reinforcing the idea that long-term grape consumption may enhance muscle function. These findings suggest that grapes could have broader health benefits, particularly in modulating muscle physiology in a sex-dependent manner.
